critic_raw:
This is a carefully designed methodology with explicit temporal splitting, campaign deduplication, training-only feature fitting, and honest scoping statements. Most engineering-level concerns are addressed. I'll focus on the genuine methodological risks that remain.

**Issue 1: The 14-day adjudication window creates asymmetric label noise with temporal structure**

The specific claim: Emails confirmed malicious within 14 days are reliable positives; messages never escalated are reliable negatives.

Why it might be wrong: Analyst confirmation latency is not uniform across the 12-month period. Early in the dataset, more emails have had time to be retrospectively escalated (e.g., a campaign recognized in month 6 may trigger re-review of month 2 messages). Near the test boundary (months 11-12), some true positives will have their confirmation delayed past the cutoff window — they appear as negatives during labeling but would have been positives with more time. This produces a systematic false-negative inflation in the test set, which deflates recall estimates and biases PR-AUC downward in the test period relative to earlier months. The direction of bias is conservative (understates model quality), but it means the validation-period label quality is better than the test-period label quality, which corrupts the model selection criterion: the best checkpoint on validation is selected under cleaner labels than the checkpoint will be evaluated against. More subtly, if the model learns that low-confidence-looking emails in the final weeks of a period tend to be unlabeled, it could be systematically miscalibrated at the temporal boundary.

Evidence: Compare label completion rates (fraction of positives confirmed within each day of the 14-day window) across the three partitions. If month 11-12 positives skew toward day 13-14 confirmations at a higher rate than earlier months, the effect is real. A sensitivity analysis labeling test-set positives with a 7-day cutoff vs. 14-day cutoff and checking PR-AUC stability would quantify the magnitude.

**Issue 2: Campaign cluster assignment by first appearance conflates two distinct leakage risks**

The specific claim: Assigning each cluster wholly to the partition containing its first appearance prevents the model from seeing one copy of a phishing wave during training and another in test.

Why it might be wrong: Assigning entire clusters by first appearance means a campaign that begins in month 7 (late train) and resurfaces in month 11 (test) is correctly kept out of test — good. But a campaign that begins in month 9 (validation) and resurfaces in month 11 (test) will be entirely assigned to validation, removing it from test. This does prevent leakage, but it also means the test set is depleted of recurring campaigns, making it systematically more novel than what the model will face in real deployment (where some fraction of threats are known variants). PR-AUC on this artificially novelty-heavy test set will underestimate real-world performance on recurring campaigns — again a conservative bias — but it means the 'previously unseen campaign clusters' slice IS the entire test set, so that ablation collapses into the main evaluation and loses its diagnostic value. You cannot separately attribute performance to generalization on novel vs. variant campaigns if the test set contains only novel ones.

Evidence: Measure what fraction of test-period messages (by volume, not cluster count) belong to clusters that have no prior appearance in train or validation. If this approaches 100%, the slice analysis is degenerate. The fix is to distinguish between 'cluster first seen before month 9' (recurring) and 'cluster first seen month 11-12' (novel) within the test set, then report recall separately on each.

**Issue 3: The gradient-boosted baseline excludes free text, making the comparison test two confounded hypotheses simultaneously**

The specific claim: The baseline excludes free text so the experiment directly tests whether message language plus structured context adds detection value beyond conventional gateway features.

Why it might be wrong: The comparison conflates modality (text vs. no text) with architecture (transformer fine-tuning vs. gradient boosting). If the transformer outperforms the baseline, the gain could be attributed to (a) the language signal, (b) the inductive bias of attention over structured features, (c) pretrained world knowledge in the transformer backbone, or (d) the representation learning over structured features in the joint head. The ablation that removes body text from the transformer helps partially, but if the text-ablated transformer still beats the GBT, the experiment shows that transformer-style processing of structured features is itself valuable — which is a different finding than 'language adds value.' The authors acknowledge this is testing whether 'message language plus structured context adds detection value,' which is technically true but obscures which component is responsible. For a security team deciding whether to pay the inference cost and interpretability cost of a transformer, this conflation matters operationally.

Evidence: Add a third model: a transformer (or MLP) trained only on the structured features with no text, using the same architecture as the classification head. If this closes most of the gap to the full multimodal model, the marginal value of language is small. If the GBT and structured-only transformer perform similarly, the gain is attributable to language. Without this cell, the ablation table is underdetermined.

**Issue 4: Reputation features are point-in-time snapshots but are subject to look-ahead contamination in practice**

The specific claim: Point-in-time reputation logs are included as features, and all transformations are fit on training only.

Why it might be wrong: Point-in-time reputation scores require that the reputation system was actually running and logging at receipt time. In practice, reputation feeds are often backfilled, retroactively updated, or subject to API versioning changes. If the reputation snapshot for a domain at month 11 reflects reputation data computed using month 9-10 incident data (as many commercial threat-intel feeds work, with weekly or daily recalculation over rolling windows), then the reputation feature for test-set messages contains implicit future information. This is not a training/test split issue — it's a feature construction issue that temporal splitting does not resolve. The mechanism is: attacker domain is flagged by the reputation feed after being seen in month 9 campaigns; the feed's retroactive scoring then marks that domain as malicious in all historical snapshots including the month 11 messages where it reappears.

Evidence: Audit the reputation feed provenance — specifically, whether historical snapshots are immutable or whether the feed provider updates historical records. If snapshots are mutable, the contamination risk is real. A direct test: for domains that first appeared in training, compare reputation scores as logged at receipt time versus scores queryable today; systematic retrospective inflation is a red flag.

**Summary of verdict reasoning:**

Issues 1 and 4 are genuine methodological risks with specific failure mechanisms. Issue 1 (label timing asymmetry) has the potential to corrupt model selection in a directionally consistent but measurable way. Issue 4 (reputation look-ahead) is a latent data-quality assumption that the methodology assumes away without verification. Issue 2 is a design tension that makes the planned slice analysis degenerate. Issue 3 is a comparison design gap that makes the primary scientific claim (language adds value) underdetermined. None of these are fatal to the entire experiment, but issues 3 and 4 together mean the primary conclusion — that the multimodal transformer's gains are attributable to learned language — cannot be cleanly supported by this design as stated.
all_issues_raised: The 14-day adjudication window creates systematic false-negative inflation in the test set due to confirmation latency, which differs structurally from the validation set and corrupts the model selection criterion.

Assigning entire campaign clusters to the partition of first appearance depletes the test set of recurring campaigns, making the 'previously unseen clusters' ablation slice degenerate (it collapses into the full test set).

The baseline design conflates modality (text presence) with architecture (GBT vs. transformer), leaving the primary scientific claim — that language adds detection value — underdetermined without a structured-only transformer comparison cell.
Point-in-time reputation snapshots may be retroactively updated by commercial feeds, introducing look-ahead contamination that temporal splitting does not address.

Label construction is biased toward detectable/acted-upon attacks: sophisticated phishing that evades analyst confirmation within 14 days is excluded as unlabeled, enriching the positive class with easier cases and potentially overstating detection performance on the hardest targets.

The negative class reliability is temporally confounded: changes in analyst capacity or thoroughness between the training and test periods can create directional label noise aligned with the temporal split.

The GBT baseline and the 'transformer minus text' ablation use different structured feature sets, so any performance gap cannot be cleanly attributed to text language versus architectural differences or feature richness differences.

PR-AUC is sensitive to the positive rate in the test window; if months 11-12 contain an anomalous attack campaign or takedown, the PR-AUC estimate has high variance and the comparison to the baseline may be unreliable.

Point-in-time reputation correctness is asserted but not validated by a described audit; if reputation snapshots reflect post-confirmation state for any training examples, positive-class performance is inflated.
14-day analyst confirmation window enriches the positive class with detectable phishing, creating label selection bias correlated with model signal strength.
Mixing bulk/spam and benign as a combined negative class creates a heterogeneous decision boundary that may inflate performance if the spam/benign ratio shifts across the temporal split.

Pretrained transformer weights may encode phishing campaign knowledge from pretraining corpora independently of local fine-tuning, creating an asymmetric advantage over the GBT baseline that the comparison design cannot isolate.
Point-in-time reputation features are partially a function of training-period mail volume for known senders, creating a smooth cold-start degradation that PR-AUC on the full test set obscures.
PR-AUC is sensitive to positive prevalence, so validation-period model selection and test-period evaluation are not directly comparable if confirmed-phishing prevalence shifts between periods.

assessor_results.critic_raw:
This is a well-constructed experimental design. Let me work through each potential issue systematically to find genuine flaws versus imagined ones.

**1. Label construction via 14-day analyst confirmation window**

The claim: messages confirmed malicious within 14 days are reliable positives; messages resolved benign and never later escalated are reliable negatives.

The concern: Analyst confirmation is not a uniform process. Spear-phishing emails that were opened, acted upon, and caused a breach are likely to be confirmed quickly and thoroughly. Sophisticated spear-phishing that was never opened or that caused no downstream observable incident may never be confirmed at all — it falls into the 'unlabeled' exclusion bucket. This creates a systematic bias: the positive class is enriched with *caught* or *acted-upon* attacks, not representative of the full attack distribution. The model learns to recognize the detectable fraction of phishing, not phishing per se.

The negatives have a symmetric issue: a message 'resolved benign and never later escalated' is only as reliable as analyst capacity. If analysts are backlogged in months 1-8 but more thorough in months 9-12 (e.g., due to a security incident or staffing change), the test-set negative label quality is systematically different from training. This isn't just noise — it's a directional confound aligned with the temporal split.

Evidence one way or the other: Compute the proportion of labeled positives versus total inbound volume by month. If it's flat, labeling rate is stable. If it trends, the negative class definition is contaminated by temporal changes in analyst behavior. Also compare the time-to-confirmation distribution across the train/val/test partitions.

**2. Cluster-based deduplication and the definition of 'campaign variant'**

The claim: assigning clusters wholly to one partition based on first appearance prevents a model from seeing one copy of a phishing wave during training and another in test.

The concern: This is correct in intent, but the clustering criteria (normalized body similarity, shared URL infrastructure, subject/header overlap) will fail to capture semantic campaign variants that change infrastructure or language while retaining operational intent. Sophisticated actors rotate domains and rewrite bodies. The real risk isn't near-duplicate leakage (which the deduplication addresses) — it's that the training set contains 'easy' earlier campaign variants and the test set contains evolved harder variants. This is not leakage; it's the actual distribution shift the model must handle. The methodology does not conflate these two issues, but the deduplication procedure as described only solves the leakage problem, not the drift problem. That's fine — the authors acknowledge drift — but the campaign-level recall metric reported post-deduplication may understate the difficulty of novel campaign detection because novel campaigns are defined by absence of training-set cluster membership, not by actual novelty of tactic.

Evidence: Report campaign-level recall stratified by campaign cluster size. If large clusters (many variants, more training signal) dominate recall and novel single-instance clusters have near-zero recall, the model is memorizing campaign signatures rather than generalizing to phishing tactics.

**3. Transformer text features and the structured metadata baseline split**

The claim: the GBT baseline uses engineered metadata only, excluding free text, so the experiment tests whether message language adds value beyond conventional gateway features.

This is a reasonable experimental design for a directional question. However, there is a subtle confound: the transformer fine-tuning also ingests structured features concatenated with the text representation. The ablation that removes body text from the transformer is presented as the counterfactual, but this ablation is not the same model as the GBT baseline — it differs in architecture, feature engineering, and training procedure. So 'transformer minus text' versus 'GBT on metadata' is not a clean comparison. The GBT baseline likely has different and potentially richer hand-engineered features than what is concatenated to the transformer head. A gain from the full multimodal transformer over the GBT could come from (a) language, (b) the interaction between language and structured features that the concatenation learns, (c) better optimization of the same feature set due to the neural architecture, or (d) some combination. The ablations partially address this but do not close the gap.

The methodology explicitly disclaims that this proves robustness across organizations, which is appropriate. But within-organization attribution of the gain mechanism is still underspecified by the described ablation set.

Evidence: Add a GBT trained on the same structured features that are concatenated to the transformer head, not just 'conventional gateway features.' This isolates whether the transformer text representation is doing work versus whether the structured feature set fed to the transformer is richer than what the GBT baseline receives.

**4. PR-AUC and class imbalance with unstable label counts**

The claim: PR-AUC captures triage quality across realistic alert volumes under extreme class imbalance.

PR-AUC is indeed appropriate here. The one genuine concern is that PR-AUC is sensitive to the positive rate in the evaluation set, and if the test period (months 11-12) happens to include a phishing wave or campaign takedown that shifts the positive rate relative to training, the PR-AUC value is not directly comparable to a steady-state deployment. This is a known limitation of PR-AUC in event-driven security contexts. The authors mitigate this partially by also reporting precision@500 and precision@1000, which are rank-based and less sensitive to base rate, but do not explicitly flag this PR-AUC instability risk.

Evidence: Report the positive rate by month across all twelve months. If months 11-12 are anomalous in attack volume, the PR-AUC estimate has high variance and the comparison to the GBT baseline is noisy.

**5. Reputation feature temporality**

The claim: reputation logs are 'point-in-time' at receipt, preventing look-ahead bias.

This is correctly flagged and the most common silent misconfiguration in this type of experiment. The methodology asserts it is handled. The failure mode would be if reputation snapshots are stored as current-state rather than historical state — i.e., if a sender's reputation was updated after an analyst confirmed the message as malicious, and that updated reputation is what gets joined to the training example. This would inflate performance substantially on the positive class.

The description asserts point-in-time reputation is used, so if implemented correctly this is not an issue. But this is precisely the kind of claim that is easy to state and hard to verify without inspecting the data pipeline. The ablation that removes reputation features is a partial check — if performance drops substantially when reputation is removed, and reputation is known to lag confirmation decisions, that warrants scrutiny.

Evidence: Check timestamp ordering: for every training example, verify that the reputation snapshot timestamp precedes the message receipt timestamp. Also check whether reputation values for confirmed-malicious senders in the training set match their pre-confirmation versus post-confirmation state.

**Overall assessment:**

The methodology is substantially more rigorous than the typical enterprise email security paper. The temporal split, cluster deduplication, train-only transformation fitting, ablation design, and metric choice are all appropriate. The issues I've identified are:
- Issue 1 (label quality bias toward detectable/acted-upon attacks) is a genuine structural concern that could cause performance to be overestimated on the positive class as actually deployed.
- Issue 3 (confounded attribution between language and structured feature richness) is real but partially mitigated and the authors don't overclaim.
- Issues 2, 4, 5 are real but the methodology addresses them in ways that make them residual risks rather than fundamental flaws.

The most significant unaddressed issue is Issue 1: the labeling process systematically excludes the hardest positive cases (sophisticated spear-phishing that evaded detection for more than 14 days, or that analysts did not confirm), and this is structural, not fixable by the described controls.

issues_raised: 14-day analyst confirmation window enriches the positive class with detectable phishing, creating label selection bias correlated with model signal strength.
Mixing bulk/spam and benign as a combined negative class creates a heterogeneous decision boundary that may inflate performance if the spam/benign ratio shifts across the temporal split.

Pretrained transformer weights may encode phishing campaign knowledge from pretraining corpora independently of local fine-tuning, creating an asymmetric advantage over the GBT baseline that the comparison design cannot isolate.
Point-in-time reputation features are partially a function of training-period mail volume for known senders, creating a smooth cold-start degradation that PR-AUC on the full test set obscures.
PR-AUC is sensitive to positive prevalence, so validation-period model selection and test-period evaluation are not directly comparable if confirmed-phishing prevalence shifts between periods.
assessor_results.critic_raw:
This is a well-constructed methodology with explicit temporal splitting, duplicate/campaign control, appropriate metric choice, and honest scope limitations. I'll work through the genuine issues I can identify.

**Issue 1: The 14-day analyst confirmation window creates asymmetric label noise that is correlated with the model's target signal.**

The claim: Positives confirmed within 14 days are reliable ground truth. The mechanism of failure: analyst confirmation is not independent of the email's surface features. Analysts are more likely to escalate and confirm messages that look obviously malicious (clear phishing language, known bad domains, flagged attachments). Subtle or novel spear-phishing that successfully evades analyst attention within 14 days gets silently absorbed into the 'unlabeled' exclusion bucket or, worse, into the negative pool if it was resolved as benign. This creates a systematic bias: the positive class is enriched with detectable phishing, and the model learns to score against a label distribution that is easier than production reality. The ablation removing body text partially probes this, but won't expose it. Evidence for this: compute the false negative rate on messages initially triaged as benign but later escalated after 14 days (even if excluded from training, this population exists and can be scored post-hoc). If the model scores these late-confirmed positives substantially lower than the 14-day-confirmed positives, the label selection mechanism is biasing the positive class toward easier cases.

**Issue 2: The negative class construction conflates two populations with different distributional properties, and the 'never later escalated' criterion may not hold under concept drift.**

The claim: Messages resolved as benign or bulk/spam and never escalated are clean negatives. The mechanism of failure: 'bulk/spam' and 'benign' are not the same class. Bulk/spam has distinct surface features (unsubscribe links, marketing structure, high-volume sender patterns) that differ systematically from the benign transactional or internal mail. A model trained on this mixed negative class learns a decision boundary that partially separates phishing from spam *and* phishing from benign — but in deployment, the relevant boundary is phishing vs. all non-phishing. If the test set has a different spam/benign ratio than training (plausible across months given seasonal mail patterns), PR-AUC on the test set may not reflect production performance. Evidence: stratify the negative class in validation results by sub-type (spam vs. benign internal vs. benign external) and check whether the model's score distribution shifts across these sub-populations. A robust model should assign similarly low scores to all negatives; a model exploiting spam-specific surface features will show a secondary score mode at spam.

**Issue 3: The cluster-then-split procedure prevents message-level leakage but may not prevent the transformer from benefiting from campaign-infrastructure knowledge embedded in the pretrained weights.**

The claim: Assigning clusters to partitions by first appearance eliminates data leakage between train and test. This is correct for the supervised signal. The subtler issue: a pretrained transformer fine-tuned on months 1-8 will encode phishing language patterns from its pretraining corpus, which may include public reporting, threat intelligence feeds, or web-crawled content that post-dates some test-period campaigns. This is not leakage in the conventional sense, but it means the transformer's apparent generalization to 'novel' test-period campaigns may partly reflect prior exposure to those campaign templates in the pretraining data rather than genuine transfer from local fine-tuning. The GBT baseline does not benefit from this, making the comparison asymmetric in a way that favors the transformer beyond what local training data alone would justify. Evidence: compare fine-tuned transformer vs. zero-shot (frozen) transformer on the test set. If zero-shot performance is non-trivially high on test-period campaign clusters marked as 'previously unseen,' pretraining exposure is contributing independently of local fine-tuning.

**Issue 4: Reputation features create a temporal integrity risk that the train/test split design does not fully address.**

The claim: Using 'point-in-time' reputation logs ensures no future reputation signal bleeds into training features. This is operationally correct but incomplete. The issue is that reputation scores for senders/domains/IPs active during months 1-8 will have accumulated signal from exactly those months' mail activity. If a campaign is large enough to move a domain's reputation score during training, the reputation feature is partially a function of the volume of malicious mail from that domain — meaning reputation is a leaky proxy for 'this domain appeared in many confirmed malicious messages during training.' For new domains in months 11-12 (the 'previously unseen sender domains' slice), reputation features are cold-start and the model must rely more heavily on language. The slice analysis proposed does probe this, but the confound isn't just about seen vs. unseen — it's that reputation quality degrades smoothly with recency, which PR-AUC on the full test set obscures. Evidence: within the test set, plot PR-AUC as a function of sender domain age (days since first appearance in training data). Genuine language-based generalization should show a smaller performance cliff at the cold-start boundary than a reputation-dependent model.

**Issue 5: PR-AUC as primary metric is appropriate for the stated objective but has a known instability property under the class ratio shifts that temporal splitting induces.**

The claim: PR-AUC captures triage quality across realistic alert volumes under class imbalance. This is correct in a static setting. The mechanism of concern: PR-AUC is sensitive to the positive prevalence in the evaluation set. If the fraction of confirmed phishing in months 11-12 differs from months 1-8 (plausible: either attacker activity spikes or analyst capacity bottlenecks reduce confirmation rate), the PR-AUC values across periods are not directly comparable, and the validation-period PR-AUC used for model selection may not select the checkpoint that maximizes test-period PR-AUC. The methodology already flags this indirectly via 'recall at fixed alert-volume budgets' and 'precision in top 500/1000,' which are threshold-dependent but prevalence-stable in their operational interpretation. This issue does not invalidate the design but means the PR-AUC comparison between models on the test set could be misleading if prevalence shifted; the fixed-budget metrics are more reliable for the stated operational claim. Evidence: report positive prevalence (confirmed phishing / total labeled) separately for val and test periods. If they differ by more than ~30% relative, weight the fixed-budget recall/precision metrics more heavily than PR-AUC in the comparison.

**Summary assessment:** Issues 1 and 3 are the most structurally significant. Issue 1 (label selection bias) means the positive class may be systematically easier than production positives, inflating all models' performance. Issue 3 (pretraining asymmetry) means the transformer vs. GBT comparison may not purely test 'does learned language help' but also 'does a pretrained model carry external knowledge of phishing.' The proposed ablations (remove body text, slice on unseen campaigns) partially address Issue 3 but do not resolve it. The cluster-based split, temporal partitioning, and explicit scope disclaimer are genuine strengths. This is a methodologically serious design with two non-trivial confounds that could meaningfully affect the interpretation of the main result.
